name: Cova de ses Meravelles
clothing: Coveralls recommended if accessing all corners
distance: 1 km from parking next to Coma-sema house
coordinates_utm: DATUM EUROPEAN 1950: 481363 E, 4400340 N. DATUM ETRS89: 481260 E, 4400140 N
description: A 500 m long cave once developed for tourist visits, featuring remnants of stone stairways enabling an easy circular tour. It contains numerous chambers including the Sala des Blocs (an ancient necropolis), elaborate speleothems, and the spectacular Enchanted Castle area with pure white active stalagmites. The deepest point is -35 m in the Clay Chamber, and the cave is on private property requiring landowner permission to visit.
full_description:
Description of the cavity

We find ourselves before a cavity with a development of about 500 m, which was once fitted out for visits, so although those works are no longer in the same state of preservation as then, they do allow for an easy visit with minimal precaution (the cave is very slippery after a rainy day). Once the Boca des Forn (Oven's Mouth) is crossed, the design of the conditioning works allows for a circular visit of the cave.

To access the interior of the cavity, one must descend via some old stone stairs until reaching a gate which, once crossed and after descending eleven more steps, places us on a kind of landing from which we can access the Sala des Mort (Hall of the Dead) to our right.

Sala des Mort (Hall of the Dead)

Small hall of 13 x 13 x 8 m located in a bend of the entrance stairway. It is accessed through a crawlway of slightly less than 0.40 m in height that had to be cleared at some point. Once inside, we find a horizontal hall somewhat decorated with speleothems though with little activity.

Noteworthy in this hall is the existence of two "chimneys" (vertical pits) on the northern side, reasonably decorated with concretions. Their verticality and slippery surface make their exploration impossible.

This hall, like the Sala des Blocs (Hall of Blocks), served as a necropolis for the ancient inhabitants of the mountain range.

Sala des Blocs (Hall of Blocks)

This is a structural hall, approximately 26 x 32 x 7 m, in which significant collapses have occurred, so its main characteristic is the presence of a chaos of blocks, more or less planar and of large dimensions.

As mentioned previously, the ceiling features the presence of rocky strata of great continuity, with tepee morphologies.

The ornamentation of this cave is restricted to the southern zone, where there is a ramped area with abundant columns of small dimensions, through which one can access the upper part of the Sala de s'Argila (Clay Hall); and to the western side of the hall, where large wall flowstones have developed.

A large part of this hall was used as a burial ground as attested by a great quantity of ceramic, bone, and charcoal remains that cover the floor between the blocks. Currently these remains appear disturbed and mixed. The existence of a sieve and a trowel as well as a dustpan hidden in a crack on the eastern side of the hall, behind some stone blocks, seem to confirm archaeological work of which we have no information.

In the same area, east of the hall, one can appreciate a beautiful column that formed due to water seepage through what was once a "chimney."

Saleta des Mal Pas (Little Hall of the Bad Step)

Its access is found on the north wall of the Sala des Blocs. A window above a flowstone places us above a crawlway of 5 m in length. At its end is this hall of approximately 2 x 5 m, occupied in its central area by an old flowstone.

Sala de Sa Rampa (Hall of the Ramp)

The connection between the Sala de Sa Rampa and the Sala des Blocs is made through a small gallery in the southern zone, which has been artificially enlarged (the use of drill holes can be observed), called Boca des Forn.

This is a hall of 16 x 15 x 4 m which, as its name indicates, is characterized by a more or less uniform ramp of approximately 30° slope, along which the path runs at the highest part. It is bounded at its upper part and on the northern side by flowstones called "Els Orgues" (The Organs), while on its southern side there is a curtain of columns separating it from the rest of the cave. A stairway carved into the ramp descends eastward to the deepest zones of the cavity (Sala de s'Argila, Sala de Sa Troneta, and Sala des Ribell).

As previously noted, the undulating ceiling with striations corresponds to a spectacular fault mirror, draped with numerous isolated stalactites that have been decapitated.

The thickness of the flowstone covering the ramp is not very great and in some places the red clays that constitute the substrate are exposed.

Sala EST

Located above the Sala de Sa Rampa, its dimensions are 14 x 22 x 3 m.

This is a stepped ramp hall, bounded to the north by wall flowstones and separated from the Sala de Sa Rampa by a barrier of columns and flowstones. It is on one of these northern flowstones where a graffiti appears indicating the group that first surveyed the cavity and the year it was done: EST 69, and one of the eastern flowstones with a bell-shaped morphology and blackish color is called "Sa Carretilla" (The Wheelbarrow).

Inside, notable features include the presence of gours and the existence of a small, heavily concretioned hall at the southwest end. It is in this hall where one can observe a stalagmitic formation known as "Sa Ma des Gegant" (The Giant's Hand) and the presence of a small chimney.

Additionally, the abundant fault mirrors that can be observed on the ceiling of the lower part of the hall are worthy of mention.

Central zone of the cavity

From the EST Hall or ascending from the Sala des Ribell, we will arrive at the central part of the Cova de Ses Meravelles. This is a flat area where only the presence of two large stalagmites stands out. If we come from the EST Hall, we will have climbed a small ramp on whose right side we can observe the column known as "Es Pop" (The Octopus), which presents a whitish color that stands out against the rest.

From this area, ascending a small ramp to the northwest we will reach the Racó d'en Miquel; if we ascend another steeper ramp to the southwest, we will reach Sa Ximeneia (The Chimney); or if we prefer, climbing some very steep stairs we will reach Es Castell Encantat (The Enchanted Castle).

Racó d'en Miquel (Miguel's Corner)

To access its interior, one must cross a very narrow gallery of 5 m in length, once the top of the small ramp to the northwest of the central zone of the cavity has been reached. At that point, the gallery entrance, there are some deep gours.

This is a small room of only 2 x 2 m corresponding to an ancient gour, where a great quantity of phreatic speleothems of great purity stand out. The name comes from the graffiti still preserved in this place.

Sa Xemeneia (The Chimney)

This is a spectacular pit of large dimensions, with no apparent connection to the outside, although it must be very close to the surface.

The diameter of the chimney's mouth is about 4.0 m, narrowing as it ascends. Its interior is covered with flowstones that hang from its mouth and indicate that there has been a significant water input through it. For all of this, it can be stated that it has almost certainly played a very important role in the development of the cave.

Nearby, a "disc" is located on the ceiling — a secondary speleothem that forms when water exits the rock under some pressure while simultaneously precipitating calcite particles on each side.

Saleta des Fang (Little Hall of Mud)

Just half a dozen meters from the chimney, toward the north, there is a small room accessed through a narrow passage carved in the rock that overcomes a subvertical flowstone.

This flat-ceilinged room is hidden just below a large flowstone. The room can only be accessed by crawling and at no point can one stand upright.

This is a room of 5 x 12 x 1.5 m, completely flat, decorated by several columns and wall flowstones, and whose floor is completely covered in mud, from which its name derives.

Sala des Gours (Hall of Gours)

Ascending from the Sa Ximeneia area toward the west, we will reach this hall, located in the highest part of the cavity. The dimensions of this hall are 21 x 27 x 6 m. The hall can be divided into two zones: the southwestern half corresponds to a ramp of gentle slope that ends in a fairly open and flat area carpeted with mini-gours; the northeastern half corresponds to a large-scale flowstone crowned by abundant gours of small dimensions but deep, and bounded at its northeastern end by columns.

The cavity ends at its western end where a vertical flowstone exists.

Sala de Ses Arrels (Hall of Roots)

In the northwest area of the Sala des Gours, behind the final flowstone, is this small hall of 4 x 9 x 2.5 m, accessed through a low-ceilinged entrance. The presence of roots, from which it takes its name, is notable.

To the right of its entrance stands out a large curtain whose interior is draped with numerous eccentric stalactites — perhaps the most beautiful part of the entire cave.

Es Castell Encantat (The Enchanted Castle)

Descending from the Sala des Gours, if we stay close to the southwest wall, we can reach the Es Pulpit (The Pulpit) area — a small "balcony" formed by stalagmites.

Stairs will allow us to descend along the southwest side and we will arrive at the upper area of the Castell Encantat, where the first thing that catches our attention will be the existence of three active stalagmites of pure white, as if they were ghosts standing out against the black of the rest of the cavity.

Descending a bit more we will arrive at a flat area where we can either continue descending to the southeast toward the Sala de l'Infern (Hall of Hell), or descend via vertical stairs to the central zone of the cave from where we can begin our exit from the cavity.

This area stands out for the abundance of columnar formations and flowstones, being the most concretioned zone of the entire cavity. Being located in a high area of the cavity, it appears as a fortress, a castle situated on top of a promontory — hence its name.

Sala de l'Infern (Hall of Hell)

This hall can only be reached from the Es Castell Encantat area, via descending vertical stairs, so once visited we must retrace our steps.

This is a hall of 11 x 22 m located in the southern zone of the cave, above the Sala des Ribell (Basin Hall). The latter is observable from a kind of balcony in the lower part of the hall. Like Es Castell Encantat, it stands out for the great abundance of column and flowstone type speleothems.

Sala des Ribell (Basin Hall)

From the central zone of the cavity, descending eastward along a ramp of undulating morphology, steeper than the one in the Sala de Sa Rampa, we will arrive at this hall.

The approximate dimensions of this hall are 20 x 22 x 6 m. A ribell (ceramic basin) is placed in a small depression in the center of the hall. Said basin is always filled with crystalline water from which cave visitors drink, as evidenced by the existence of some cups hidden in a nearby crack. In its vicinity, a beautiful nest of pisoliths can be observed.

Additionally, notable features include the existence of several isolated stalagmites of approximately 1.0 m in height around the basin and a small sinkhole filled with clays at the southwest end.

Sala de Sa Troneta (Sinkhole Hall)

Crossing the curtain of columns heading north, we will arrive at the lower part of the Sala de Sa Rampa and descending some steps we will reach the hall.

This hall functions as the sinkhole of the cave, collecting all the water that enters it. The dimensions are 6 x 12 x 2.5 m. The southern area houses a great quantity of small, clean formations that the mud flooding the hall has not managed to reach.

Saleta Amagada (Hidden Little Hall)

This small hall of approximately 2.5 x 5 x 2 m is found at the back of the Sala de Sa Troneta. Prior to the clearing work performed, it was hidden behind the mud. This is a beautiful hall that houses beautiful formations in an extraordinary state of preservation. One should not enter its interior so as not to dirty it, having to be content with contemplating it from the cleared "window."

Sala de S'Argila (Clay Hall)

It is found after the Sala de Sa Troneta; before reaching it, we will leave to our right a small 2.5 m deep well that possibly also functions as a sinkhole (troneta) and marks the deepest point of the cavity at -35 m.

The dimensions of the hall are 10 x 21 x 3 m, the surface being completely flat since in this part of the cave significant thicknesses of red decalcification clays have accumulated. The only notable features are the existence of a large gour with a flat bottom in the eastern part of the hall and the tepee morphology of the ceiling.

Once the visit is complete, one need only climb the stairs located on the north side of the Sala de Sa Rampa ramp and cross the Boca des Forn to our right.

Note: The Cova de ses Meravelles is on private property, so it is necessary to contact the landowner in order to visit it.
